transcript.transcript: Up the questions as well. OK. My name is Donna Wnuck, and I am here at 330 Park with Cindy Lloyd, who is going to be our narrator for today. Cindy, how are you doing? I'm doing well. How are you? Good. Good. OK, so you've had a chance to see the questions ahead of time. We're basically going to talk about family, background, how you grew up, a little bit about your career. And then we're going to focus on some Bloomfield specific kinds of things, how long you've been here, what made you come, or what made you stay, that kind of stuff. And then at the end, I'll ask you if there's anything else that we didn't talk about that you think is useful, important, or that you'd like to share. Okay, so let's start with your family background. And tell me a little bit about your family history. Now, you were just telling me about some aunts that have passed, which I'm so sorry has been happening to you and your family. If you take me back to when you were growing up, what was the family like at that point? So in Connecticut, My mother and father moved here from New Jersey. My father had previously been in law enforcement in New Jersey for Port Authority and came to Bloomfield looking to pursue those opportunities here for acquiring a lifestyle. He did indeed, after getting a job with Pratt & Whitney for a very short time, landed a job with Bloomfield Police Department. So I followed him in that endeavor as we know. And my mother, she was in clerical work, as well as rose up to the levels of executive assistant for a president at the Hartford. So both of them, at least, which was kind of really acceptable in the day, junior college educated or two years associates. But they came here for a quieter life and they found that in Bloomfield. It was the place that they thought they wanted to raise their children and it worked out the way they would have wanted it to. That's great. Yeah. And tell me about where you are in the birth order of your family. Do you have siblings? Yep. So I have a brother and I am the big sister. And I do have from my father, from previous relationship, other siblings as well that I've come to know more in my latter years than in my beginning years. So within my immediate household it was my brother and I, both growing up in Bloomfield and very competitive with each other. How old were you? Were you born in Bloomfield? I was, I'm born and raised in Bloomfield, as was my brother, so yep, I'm a day one-er. Okay. Good. And you were competitive, so tell me, Tell me about that. What was that like? Well, I loved to play with my friends and jump double dutch and hopscotch and the dolls. But living in a house with a brother, he was not playing with dolls. So we found ways to play with each other, which certainly included sports and games and things of that nature. So that's where the competitive comes in. I was on the Mattel, the Matchbox cars, the racetracks and all that stuff, playing baseball, even touch football with the boys in the park. Hence probably my ease of transition into the police department, which my brother takes credit for. He claims he made me scrappy enough for that. Seriously, no kidding. So yeah, just in our household it was the two of us and a mother and a father. Yeah, okay. And so tell me, from the early days and beyond, what kinds of dreams did your parents have for you? What did your family hope you might become? A responsible, respectful, positive contributor to society. They never put any barriers, limitations, their own desires for us on us. They allowed us to endeavor the great education that was Bloomfield in the late 60s and 70s when we were born And to carve out our own path, they just wanted us to be productive, responsible adults. And we did that. That's great. And what dreams did you have for yourself? What did you think you'd be when you grew up? I had no idea. I did not. And so this is a story that I've just recently started telling. Everyone believed because I was a police officer in Bloomfield that I just wanted to follow my dad's footsteps and I did turn out to be a legacy but it was really happenstance. I learned at the time I was going through college and money was a challenge that police departments and other businesses paid tuition reimbursement And so I had an aha moment. I said, what? Really? So I started applying everywhere, including state police. And Bloomfield was the first place that offered me a job. And so I thought I might get my education wrapped up and figure out what I wanted to do. And once I was in there, I actually waited, man, besides taking onesie-twosie classes, I believe I waited about three or four years before I went back, hardcore, full-time, to finish getting my education. And what I discovered in that time was that I loved policing. I loved being of service to my community, and distinctly my community, because I knew the people who I was interacting with and helping on a daily basis. That's supposed to be what happens, right? It was a great gift. It was a great gift, yes. That's great. That sounds great. So this is a question about how growing up in Bloomfield, in your family as it stood, with your brother living there with you and your parents, the education that you got, potentially your religion. How did it shape your identity? I'd say, honestly, I say this frequently, that not only my family raised me, but Bloomfield raised me. I went to church with family in a traditional Christian type setting, which was fine. Religion does shape morals and values, but you don't have to be a religious person to know what good values and morals are. But it did play a role in my upbringing as well as my parents raising me. I always give credit to the Bloomfield community raised me as well. It was my teachers. It was the people when you walk in the store saying hello and and mirroring those values Everywhere we went traversing this town From in school to playing sports and learning values and sportsmanship Everywhere you went you were kind of infused with hopefully doing the right thing. It was just how the community was and generally is, even as we speak, which is why I'm still here. Mm-hmm. That's great. So tell us a little bit about, you told us a little bit about, you know, what made you begin to apply for law enforcement and then getting brought into Bloomfield and then loving it and loving the fact that not only was it the work, but it was the work in the community that you grew up with and the people that you loved. Tell us a little bit more about your career trajectory. So that's how you started and then what happened? So it was also a great springboard to do things other than traditional policing. I signed up for everything that nobody else wanted to do. Like what? Like, well, everybody wanted SWAT and firearms and I did that. And I was a firearms instructor for over nine years. But as well at the time, and I do understand from the current chief, it's different now. A lot of people want to work with the kids, but at the time I was applying, there was only a few of us. And so I applied for everything from DARE officer, Drug Abuse Resistance Education, 17-week lessons dispensed in the classroom in the middle school, as well as the first school resource officer. That was in 99. So when you say you were the first school resource officer, does that mean the first in Bloomfield, the first in the high school, or the way were you? The first in Bloomfield. So I went back and forth between the middle and the high school because we only had money for one. As well, before that, former Youth Services Director Pat Menegia put together a, it was a board for kids who were getting in trouble in school. So, we had the juvenile review board. I sat on the inaugural juvenile review board. I volunteered for anything with the kids. Let's see, what else? Police Explorer Advisor. Jeffrey Blatter and Mark Manson awakened our chapter after many years of dormancy and I became involved in that as well where we have kids who get trained in policing and from the ages of 13 to 18 they learn what we do, how we do, they wear uniforms, they ride along, they truly get an immersive experience. So that was kind of the trajectory besides the rough and tumble stuff with the firearms and the SWAT. I did anything with the kids because when you see adults coming back and forth, round and round, through the revolving door, for whatever reason is that they are getting in trouble. It's frustrating, but the kids are truly a worthy investment and I recognize that even though I was very young, I was in my 20s, but I knew that you really could impact them. And the evidence of that, albeit a little embarrassing, I'll tell this story too. As I reached my mid to late 30s, or even like early 40s, I would go out, cause I like to go out. I love dancing, I love music. And young people to me would walk up to me and I have to quote them exactly the way, yo, Miss, Officer Cindy, what's up, what's up? And I'm looking at this six foot three teddy bear and I'm like, Oh my gosh, I'm like, we're in a club. You can call me Cindy. Don't do that in here. And then, you know, we would converse about how they're doing, how many kids do they have, I'll guess their age, I'm usually within a year or two, but the faces never change. So it showed me that the impact that I had to these kids was lasting. Comments like, Miss, you're the only police officer I ever liked. Or you know, you really were impactful in my life. When you came to my house and this was going on, I'll never forget you for that. So Those are the things that I've learned actually more after the fact, after retirement, as I continue to run across these young people who are now adults, how I had, and other police officers have such a significant capacity to be impactful in the lives of their community and especially kids who you can just really try you need to try your best not to throw Them away because you never know I ran into what people perceived to be a throwaway When I was taking a real estate course in oh four I retired in oh two I was at MCC and this kid was perceived to be just a big troublemaker. They didn't bet too much for him, and he was taking classes, about to graduate with his associates. Two years after, he was kind of written off by a lot of people. So we greeted each other and like, what are you doing here? What are you doing here? And it was just a, it was a wonderful thing to see. Yeah. Yeah. He was there and he had gotten through his early adolescence and he was moving on. Yes. That sounds great. Yeah, it's true. Yeah. That's really, It does make a lot of sense. But again, especially knowing, hearing that you were one of the people during a time when people didn't recognize the importance yet of getting to kids early. Mm-hmm. There's a lot to be proud of there. Yeah, I am, I am. We did have Officer Friendly at times in the past in the police department. Yeah, how did that work? I mean, that was for the little kids, right? It was like the little, little kids, right. And so it was like you come in and you do visits, but it wasn't as intensive as a 17-week program like DARE or being in the school every single day, being someone that you want to build a rapport with the kids that there's someone that they can trust you. So it was a whole other level of impact. Even my dad worked with the youth, himself and a smaller group of guys back in the day in the 70s put together the Bloomfield PAL, Police Athletic League, which tapered off as well by the time I got there, just like the Explorers, which of course, we revived the Explorers, but PAL never quite did because other programs were available in town, so it was okay. Yeah, But that's cool. It's a cool way of, absent a formal program, taking the initiative to put that together. That's really good. That's really admirable. So talk a little bit more about your career. So you talked about how you get started and you talked about the things that you took on and the focus on youth and the value there and how it's been rewarding even these years later and potentially even more rewarding since you're now getting a sense of what you, how much you meant at the time maybe the kids are not so open about that. So in the beginning, and I do this thing, I compartmentalize now, you know, psychologists have a word for everything now. I compartmentalize where I kind of just write stuff off. It's like I don't even remember, but now you asking me a second time Has triggered the thought it was very challenging when I first entered to police department There was only three other women And I guess they had run through them as it relates to, you know, determining their worth and I was next. There was probably, definitely three, could be four, yeah, four supervisors who for their own reasons just decided they didn't want me there. One didn't like my dad, so I got leftover stuff or payback. My father was his supervisor and now I come on and he was mine. A couple of them it was because I was a female. Yeah, was it gender discrimination? Sure it was. Sure it was. Mm-hmm, absolutely. And so it was things like that. There was someone in the police department, long-standing, always got the center of attention and she found that she wasn't any longer. And so, someone that found her favorable, found her as a favorable person, what would like, kinda on her behest, you know, come for me? Just silly stuff, write-ups and all kind of things like that that found their way to the circular file every time. And after a few years of that, with the help of my dad, having some well-placed conversations with some existing supervisors saying you know she's sick of this we're just we're not doing this anymore I thought about quitting many times and then you know just the thought came over me I said I'll be darned if I let them go and give my money away to somebody else who doesn't look like me. I deserve to be here. And I had nothing but respect and great interactions with my colleagues. So, some well-placed conversations and the people that I talked to in the legal field that I'd be revisiting if it just didn't stop. And it didn't take long where, you know what, they finally, I guess, realized my value, that they couldn't shake or break me. And that was it. After that, it really was amazing. It was great. Even when they backed off, they found themselves, you know, allowing themselves to have friendly feelings toward me and treating me fine. And our interactions were great after that, but they had to get over themselves. Yeah. Yeah. So that's really interesting, that there wasn't, there didn't seem to be any residual issues between you and other people that you know. Well it could have been had I let it. Got it. What part of New Jersey, by the way? North Central. North Central. So from that experience to the city, they were living in the city and just wanted to get out of there. So. Yeah. So I'm sorry. So we talked about religion. You were talking about the pilgrimages back to New Jersey. Right. Going to church regularly there with my grandmother. That was a staple. It was a must. I wouldn't say my mom made us go as often, but you know, every time we went to visit family, we knew you better bring your Sunday best because there was no sleeping in on Sunday. As well, when we would go with my mom, dad, my aunt across the street as well. She married my uncle, very into her church. And so she was an in-law, but not an in-law to us. You could be agnostic, as long as you're getting good messaging and you're embodying it, right? Because we do have societal norms that call for us to behave in a certain fashion as well. I don't think it was as diverse as it was at the turn of the decade going into the 70s where of course it's a question but where we were dubbed all-american town, city, you know and all those things. Bloomfield was great. My mother is an amazing cook. And in addition to traditional food, Southern food, she could cook Italian food. We came to Bloomfield, we had a very strong Jewish population. She took on the community. She took on the culture of the community, not just ours. So we really opened ourselves up to the experience. I think is a good way to say it, the Bloomfield experience. Every time I have conversations that go in that direction with old friends, old classmates, people born and raised here, we have the same perception. We didn't we don't have meetings on certain days we won't if it's a religious holiday for folks as well as 9-11, right, that's a memorial for our country now, something that we honor so we don't hold events on that day or we may do an event to memorialize that significant event. We will recognize religious events and things like that, but I think we do it on a smaller level, just by recognition. We could even reach out. I've seen things where you'll give acknowledgement, right? I'll look online, Rosh Hashanah, Happy Rosh Hashanah, or Yom Kippur, or something like that, but there's no event crafted around it. And I think that's intentional. And if they did, I wouldn't be upset about it. Where some people might, because even in the most, in the recent political climate, I heard that, I don't even know, somebody made a complaint about food distribution during COVID at the church. I saw something about that. Yeah. I don't remember what happened. Something did happen. The way it's characterized and who made the calls I've learned is not how it's, not truly how it happened, but it did indeed happen, which I just think is, you know what, people in need, whoever can offer the service or the benefit or fill that need, meet that need, close the gap. Listen, it's a part of our community. We have religious-based organizations, and if they can close the gap, you know, let's work with them to meet the need. Well, and I remember what I was part of one of the distributions that was done at one of the churches and it wasn't people from the church who were there, it was that's where they they were based. It's just a nice big parking lot. It's a parking lot, exactly. There are people from all sorts of places so I can't imagine anyone objecting but perhaps that happened, I don't recall it. Yeah, Yeah. We're a little goofy. Yeah, we are a little goofy. In the 2000s. For such a great place. Yeah. And unfortunately, that's all I know. I've been here for eight years, and it's, the first few years for me, and I'm not really supposed to be talking here, but the first few years for me I worked a lot and traveled a lot. So when I retired in 2019, that's when I started paying attention and it makes a difference. You know, I have time. I want to understand what's going on in my town. I know things now that I never did. So let's talk about Bloomfield through the ages. You talked about the 70s. I saw that. Yeah, so you prepared for this. This one tends to be a struggle for some folks, but tell me what you remember. So the 70s was just, I mean, blissfully ignorant. I was a kid. I was having fun. As subconsciously diverse as I was, I had on blinders because I didn't realize that everywhere was not like this. I mean I did, you know, it was a great time because our parents trusted us. So even by the late 70s, early 80s, I'm jumping on the bus going downtown Hartford and things of that nature before all the stores moved out into malls and things like that and we just hang out and have fun and of course when you see that and you're driving through the neighborhood some of them you see a very different experience. So from that perspective and going to those places and meeting friends or meeting people from there and making friends with them, I understood the difference. So it was kind of really Mayberry-ish, right, But with diversity. It was very cool. It was really very cool. That's really good. Yeah. Yes, it was. You know, All-American City, which was great. And I believe, and you know, you guys are doing research, so correct me if I'm wrong, but as well, at one point, I was told we were dubbed somewhere like top 20 school district in the country. Check that out. Yeah, so it was just a great time to grow up in a place such as Bloomfield. The 80s, I was in high school or going into high school. I went from being a tween to a teen. I thought I was the bee's knees, you know. I'm sure you were. Cheerleader, track, you know, had a lot of friends. But again, a really interesting time of innocence, right? Just having fun, really enjoying the experience. And every experience is not without, you know, speed bumps. Bumps in a row, you know, fall down, you scrape your knees, life lessons. I don't recall, but again, my brother has a memory like a steel trap. He even had a really terrible experience in middle school that he still remembers. And it's terrible, a racist experience. It was, it's still cringe worthy to him. Where a teacher excused all of the Caucasian kids out the room and he made the black boys stay behind. He was a Caucasian teacher and he started telling them that they're what's wrong with the world, you know, and just all kind of ridiculousness like that. And He told my father, who was a cop in town, who had to restrain himself from getting arrested. Right? Because, you know. So, there were incidents like that. EC? And what would the time frame be? This would be probably in the 70s? It was. So it wasn't in the 80s, because you were in high school. Correct. And so he must have been right behind you. Yes, he was. So probably late 70s. And you know, these are the days of what we now would call politically incorrectness. Shows like Archie Bunker and stuff that I watch now and I cringe. Right? So things like that did happen. People cringed then too, but they cringed because, oh wow, he's getting away with it. Not, oh wow, that's horrible. Did you know? Yeah, yeah. That he's getting away with it. But now, fast forward, what I realized about it is though, there was a lesson. Normally, Archie walked away with a greater understanding and he evolved over the years, right? So that's what we wanna do. That's what I feel my Bloomfield education helped me to do. But having the trust of my parents to let me go and do other things in other towns and cities by ourselves, which we fear the community or society now that we can't let our kids do that. So as advanced as they are, they're still very stunted and immature and underdeveloped a lot in certain areas. So the 80s, you know, like I said, I thought I was the bee's knees. I was having a lot of fun. I had a great experience. Sports, parties, they had a lot of parties. Had a lot of house parties. It was my thing. Learning how to drive. Just all the cool stuff, you know, that you learn and you do before life hits you. Which now they call it adulting. Don't recommend. Give it two stars on Yelp. Just stay away as long as you can. So the 90s were interesting because the late 80s I became a police officer in Bloomfield. And So one thing that I recognized was the shift in the schools. And specifically noticed it even before I became heavily involved in the schools because I used to work the dances, because I like music. Oh girl, I like to party. You're gonna pay me to go listen to music? Right, cool, whatever, but to keep the kids safe, of course. And I can recall somewhere around like 92, 93, I wasn't on the force long. And I'm kind of looking at the lack of diversity in the room, the lack of diversity in the music, and then really came to realize that the diversity has slowly, slowly trickled away. No, actually rapidly, because if I was graduating in 85 and we're only at 91, 92, that's rapid, that's rapidly. It was rapid, right. So, but I just, I was in awe, like what's going on? And at the time I really wasn't tuned into the term white flight. I wasn't paying attention on that level. I just noticed it and didn't have a name for it. I just was like, wow. I knew some kids of all backgrounds that went to Northwest Catholic. Some of the Catholic schools around, those are primarily the private schools, right? A lot of them weren't going to Renbrook or Lumen. So a few of them did that type of thing. Most of us were public school kids, but I distinctly noticed in the 90s, the shift. And most of the shift was in the schools, not the community because I'm policing the community. So I'm still seeing the diversity. I'm still interacting with all different kinds of people. I'm not even gonna say like crime just went from this to this, from when I was growing up to when I first became a police officer, cause that's not even true. And it's not even true now, because Balloonfield and our neighboring community Windsor has always had the fine reputation that don't go there playing around. You'll get stopped. So don't go, as they say, riding dirty and things like that. Whatever they want to call it in the streets. But it works for us. We have an amazing omnipresence that people think you don't want to come here with a bunch of shenanigans and criminal activity because they're going to get you. And it has allowed us even to today to still have that quiet small town community experience that we want, which is what keeps us here. So the school, the shift in the schools was a big thing that I took distinct note of. But the continuity of the community itself was still there. The diversity in the community was still there, even though folks' kids were being educated elsewhere. The percentages on our diversity was still pretty good. That's interesting, yeah. Mm-hmm. And of course, had I not stayed here to work here and live here, I mean, I probably would have been none the wiser. Yeah. Unless, you know, You go to college and you leave and you come back 25 years later and you say, whoa. What happened, right? Right. Yeah. Interesting. It is. So that's 90s, 2000s. 2000s. Early 2000s. 2000s, let's say. I'm trying to think. My daughter graduated in 2010. So before she graduated, yeah. Yeah. So it just was still that community that you wanted to live in, that you could have a good quality of life. I had retired from the police department. By the mid 2000s, I was working for the state in investigations. So still a place, it was like still the place to be. There are so many of my former classmates that still live here. It's just not the place where you say, oh my God, I want to get out of there. It's really a place where folks have, many of them left and have come back because it was so significant during our formative years. And they've gone over hill and dale, far and wide, And many have come back to Bloomfield, still recognizing that it has so many of the qualities that we experienced growing up. It still gives us that feeling. Yeah, great. So this question is about race relations, specifically during the period, and you've said that you didn't, other than your brother's story, you don't remember a whole lot. I liked your quote about it wasn't about being, people didn't say stuff about being black or white, if you were a jerk, they told you you were a jerk. That pretty much sums up your experience? It really does. I mean, you always had people that would be mad at someone, and then they may just throw a racial slur or something like that. But it was not like ingrained in the culture. It just wasn't. There's other places, for example, this really took me aback. And my friend went to Tufts. And I didn't like to go visit her there because of the negative race relations in the greater Boston area. Even to the extent that around 1985 when they started integrating busing, which Bloomfield did in the 70s, early 70s. That's why we'd have kids from over here going to what was Vincent School, which is now the Museum Academy, over there because they wanted to integrate Bloomfield. So they were very... So the neighborhoods, they didn't integrate Bloomfield with other towns. No. Instead of integrating the neighborhoods. They would take someone from over there, yes, and send them over there to integrate the neighborhoods. So I'm looking at 1985 and they're really impressing the busing now, the integrated busing in Boston, and they were having race riots. And I was like, oh my God. So there's kind of another like aha moment that gave me pause and caused me to understand that I was really living in a beautiful bubble. So that goes back to the 80s but also talks about the race relations. There was certainly then, as there are now, people who are racist, sexist, homophobes, just riddled with all kinds of isms that they espouse. But it just was not any prominence or anything overt. There was certainly instances, even as of late we've had instances, particularly a couple of instances of racism that were handled very well by the council, the mayor, and the police department. I'm talking like within the last three years, four years. But those are not the norm. It's just not what and who our community is. I live in the neighborhood where the guy followed a young man who was going into the neighborhood to get paid for the lawn work, the yard work that he did. And he followed him and wanted to talk to him about why he was there. The neighbor was a white guy, former Marine. He wanted to know why the guy was there. He's like a self-proclaimed neighborhood watch guy because no one wanted him to do that. Nobody's asking him. We don't want him speaking for us and we have a very diverse neighborhood. I'm familiar with that area. How that happened was just incredible to the neighbors. And we thank God it didn't turn out to a Trayvon Martin situation. That was one of the issues. That's one of the examples that I was saying how the town handled it very well. You know, but that is few and far between is the point. And we just, you know, we have some people who are a little screwy, as we always have since the dawn of time. You know, some people think I'm a little screwy, but in a good way. Yeah. Yep. Yeah. Yeah. So, so do you know anything about the Bloomfield Nicest Town designation? I do. I want to say it was 2020 Reader's Digest, which I used to subscribe to, you know, when it was really, really popular back in the day. So I don't quite know how we were even considered for it, but I certainly concur wholeheartedly. Yeah. Yeah. I do. It just made sense to you. Of course. Yeah. Of course I had to look it up, everything. I tried to find a copy, I couldn't find a hard copy. What activities were you aware of over the years that were used to bring people together in Bloomfield? So when we talked about the 70s, the 80s, the 90s, during my policing career here, those things were in addition to for the kids, we had a lot more robust activities in the center. We had the two libraries that were very robust, but thankfully, you know, we're going to get some movement on that and bringing our libraries back to life. Because oh my gosh, going up to the library in McMahon, which is where I used to go, was not only an experience in writing a term paper, but socializing and connecting with my friends and all that kind of stuff, which nowadays the kids with the computers will do it at their desk in their house and just lose that socialization, which is so important. But we had so much going on from an economic development perspective. We always had a movie theater. I remember being in line, which it was at the old Windsor Federal. That was our movie theater when I was growing up and anxiously awaiting seeing Star Wars when it first came out. We had a bowling alley. Wintonbury Mall has been there. We had a lot more stores. It was more robust. It was like what Buckland Mall is for the kids today. It was our meetup spot and not only for the kids but like I said when I was a teen and adults. We had shopping, We had restaurants, the families would go bowling, the families would go to the movies. It seems like something small, cause you're like, you know, well, I just shoot over to Buckland. You can go over the mountains of Simsbury. They have bowling over there, a couple of movies on 44, but it's really good to be able to have those in your community. I think it think they're anchors. Yeah. And those are the things that help us to stay connected. Certainly I can tell you, Village Pizza and Elizabeth's are like anchors. Anchors, and I know when I go, I know who I'm gonna see, because it's exactly like Cheers, but the names are different, and I'm not gonna call them on the recording. No, I get it, I get it, yep. Yeah, so we still have a couple of anchors. But that is, I really miss that. Those business folks who you knew them, you knew their families. They were local. You know, they were from around here. Those are the things that distinctly I remember that we don't have now, that really tied us in. Only thing missing was a roller skating rink. Back then, We had to go to Wallingford, we had to go to Vernon. Yeah. What do you remember about Copaco? Oh, I got another story. So, Copaco, of course, is a staple. It was also a slaughterhouse in the back where you could get the freshest, best meats. I remember the Berkowitz family who were very anchored in the community, very giving and very involved in the community. My mom, being of a certain age, of course knew Miss Bonnie, who was politically active, you know, back in the time frame that my mother was. They used to have live animals in the front that you could play with them. You could ride horses. I have a nice scar on my leg to today where I got bucked and fell and some knucklehead broke some glass, you know, in the pad where the horses were and it cut right through my pants into my leg and I have a nice memory of Copacabana from that. It was definitely an anchor even back then. The McDonald's was the go to spot after all the basketball games, right? Even sometimes the football games because it used to be Saturday afternoons before we got Friday night lights. And it used to be over on the side where Boston Market was located recently that just closed. That was McDonald's right there in the corner. So yeah, Copacabana was great. I could tell you like different stores that came and left. Kim's has been there for a long time. Lim's Vitamin Shoppe used to be up closer to Stop and Shop. Now it's over on the side, but they've been there, I promise you, it's at least thirty-five, forty-five years. And I still go there for all of my holistic health, vitamins and tinctures and things like that, to make me feel better and improve and boost my health. EC? Good. Yeah, that's great. Do you have any specific knowledge about the agricultural heritage of the town? Some, just by way of being a police officer. Should I say I fell asleep in a cornfield a couple times? I don't know if I should say that. On the midnight shift. It's so true. I do know that we have a really robust, obvious agricultural history, just like most of the towns in the Farmington River Valley, being close to the water, you know, was good for growth. We have a very strong history, not only in Windsor, but in Bloomfield with Colborough tobacco. We had a lot of tobacco and things of that nature being grown, as well as just other, you know, typical agricultural staples, corn and things like that, that they grow in New England. Strawberries, blueberries, we'd go picking on the weekends, in season, and stuff like that. I know that, of course, due to development, those areas have grown, or have shrunk, excuse me. Yeah. Yeah, but you can't make anybody, you know, stick to what you want to conform to your community. Please keep your berry patch. Please, I want to bring my grandson. And so we see that now though, to bring it to today, we do see challenges with that. For example, the farm and the dairy barn, the ice cream barn. Bloom Hills. Bloom Hill on Cottage Grove Road, right? And the community doesn't wanna lose that, but they're private owners. So they should be able to retire at some point and ride into the sunset. So you know we I've seen the the farmland shrink. I know that of course we were at one point I've seen pictures of Bloomfield from the early 1900s when I don't know it couldn't have been more than 10 properties, right, on the center. And then it was just land, right, which is now all businesses, side streets, and all this kind of stuff, which is super cool. But everything develops, everything grows, and we lose our land. So on one hand I am a big proponent of good economic development to grow and bolster our towns robustness attractiveness having things to do right I don't always want to drive to Blueback. No, I want to spend my dollars here in Bloomfield. But we also have to be mindful of our land. And if we can preserve it where we can, we need to try to do so. Our bees are just, they're dying off in droves. It was 2G, now it's 3G, now it's 5G. And now, you know, I think some of these carriers are up on 10G and I've understood that those radio waves or whatever's being transmitted are part of what's negatively affecting our bees. We're losing our bees. That's how we eat, pollination. So with that said, we have to understand and be mindful of our communities and keeping that land that fosters that. Okay this is a shift. Okay. And this has to do with the challenges of housing access, discrimination, redlining, and that kind of stuff. Do you have any experience with that? Or any knowledge, even if you didn't personally experience it, any knowledge of challenges around that? I do know that, I'm trying to think of the former name, it might have been Connecticut Savings, the predecessor to Bank of America in the center that they bought them out, had had an unfortunate history in redlining and unfair practices in lending in the 80s and 90s. And So Bloomfield, as well as many, many other communities were affected by that. And it's actually distinctly a nonprofit called NACA that has been established to address that. Helping N-A-C-A to help people address those issues. They were actually the trustees of hundreds and hundreds of millions of dollars made available for lending to people who qualify, who otherwise might be turned away from the banks. And so we had a couple of those banks here in Bloomfield. So do we know how many people are negatively affected? We don't know but we know through litigation that it absolutely happened with those banks. Right? As it relates to the 80s and the 90s. Mm-hmm. And we did have a couple of those banks in Bloomfield. But I had a friend whose mom was African-American that worked many, many decades in the Bank of America in Bloomfield. So I can say that if she had any hand in it, It was gonna be a second look like, whoa, whoa, what's wrong with this application? What's wrong? So we did have some people like that that I know worked in those places, friends, parents and stuff like that, where I could probably say because of their personalities, I can tell you that it may get challenged, albeit respectfully and gently. But globally, those issues did exist and we had some of those entities here. And in Bloomfield specifically, I don't know of any redlining. I know that I personally experienced it when I was a Balloonfield police officer and I was buying a house in Windsor and the realtor Kept bringing me to the south end of Windsor down by Hartford not far from the Hartford line, And so I was qualified for a certain amount. And I actually looked in the little local paper and saw an advertisement for a new construction community up on Pequanic, not the lower end of Pequanic, the upper end. And I said, hey, why can't I look at these? Oh, I didn't think you wanted to be there. I said Windsor, I didn't say Wilson. You know what I mean? So I've had a personal experience with it, right next door. So I can say that I'm sure it has happened. Do I have any distinct proof of anybody else's experiences? Not really. Most people I know ended up where they wanted to be when it comes to buying a home in Bloomfield. Okay, I haven't heard really any bad stories about it, but we do know that that was part and parcel to the time. So, yeah. Okay. So, your story of housing in Bloomfield was when you were ready to be wherever you wanted to be here, you haven't had. And I did. Yep. I lived wherever I wanted to live. I had no problems, whether it was the purchase of a home or securing an apartment. Currently the only challenge is that it's not affordable for many people, which is a global challenge, at least in this country. It's universal. Okay, what would you like the public to know about the town? I would like the public to know that Bloomfield, for me, it still has that magical quality that I experienced growing up here, being born and raised here and being raised by this community. I love it, I don't wanna leave it. I may leave it one day, who knows? I may leave it again for love, you know? And probably end up back here before I lay my head to rest for the last time. It just has a quality that I have really not experienced in too many places. And so I think Bloomfield is close to being an anomaly. Even if we think about greater Hartford, what communities do we really have that have this feeling of such diversity? East Hartford, Manchester, but that's literally really happened over the last 10, 15 years. They did not have that. They did not. I could tell you some of the nicknames that were not good names that they had about our surrounding towns. But Blufield has always been that place. It's the place to be. That's what I want people to know. Is there anything that we haven't talked about that you think we should? No. I'm just a proud Bloomfield, born and raised, wild child, who turned out pretty good. And where I didn't do good, it was done in the dark, and I didn't get caught. That's it. KC2 All right. Well, thank you. It's been a pleasure. Lili Taylor-Johnson Thank you. Kiernan Farrow Listening and taking my notes here. Lili Taylor-Johnson Thank you.

children.text: # Introduction and Interview Outline

> Donna Wnuck introduces the interview with Cindy Lloyd and outlines the topics to be covered, including family background, career, Bloomfield-specific experiences, and any additional important topics.
** Introduction, Interview Outline, Donna Wnuck, Cindy Lloyd, Bloomfield **
# Family Background

> Cindy discusses her family history, including her parents' move from New Jersey to Bloomfield, her father's career in law enforcement, and her mother's clerical and executive assistant roles at The Hartford.
** Family Background, Parents, New Jersey, Bloomfield, Law Enforcement **
# Siblings and Childhood
> Cindy talks about her siblings, being the eldest, and her upbringing in Bloomfield, highlighting her competitive nature with her brother and her early involvement in sports and games.
** Siblings, Childhood, Competitive, Bloomfield, Sports **
# Parental Aspirations and Personal Dreams

> Discusses the aspirations Cindy's parents had for her to be a responsible and contributing adult, and how her own career path unfolded unexpectedly into law enforcement due to opportunistic educational benefits.
** Parental Aspirations, Career Path, Law Enforcement, Education **
# Identity and Community
> Cindy reflects on how growing up in Bloomfield and her family shaped her identity, emphasizing the supportive community environment and its values.
** Identity, Community, Values, Bloomfield **
# Career Trajectory in Law Enforcement
> Exploring Cindy's unexpected entry and subsequent passion for policing, her involvement with youth through programs like DARE, and becoming Bloomfield's first school resource officer.
** Career, Law Enforcement, School Resource Officer, DARE **
# Youth Engagement and Community Impact
> Cindy recounts her work with youth, including establishing programs like the juvenile review board and Police Explorer Advisor, and shares stories of lasting impact on former students.
** Youth Engagement, Community Impact, Juvenile Review Board **
# Facing Discrimination
> Cindy shares challenges she faced in the police department due to gender and personal biases, and how perseverance and self-advocacy ultimately led to professional respect and inclusion.
** Discrimination, Police Department, Perseverance, Gender Bias **
# Religious Influence and Cultural Awareness
> Discussing the influence of Christianity and exposure to diverse cultures and traditions from an early age, emphasizing the value of this multicultural foundation.
** Religion, Cultural Awareness, Christianity, Multicultural **
# Education and Cultural Awakening
> Cindy talks about her educational journey, majoring in Human Services, which deepened her cultural understanding and social awareness.
** Education, Cultural Awakening, Human Services, Springfield College **
# Generational Experiences and Community Ties
> Reflects on her family's multi-generational ties to Bloomfield and the decision to return for the community's enduring appeal despite temporary relocations.
** Generational, Community, Family Ties, Bloomfield **
# Community Involvement and Political Engagement
> Cindy shares how her parents’ civic involvement influenced her own political engagement and eventual elected position on the Bloomfield Town Council.
** Community Involvement, Political Engagement, Town Council **
# Social and Recreational Activities
> Highlights the variety of social, cultural, and recreational activities available in Bloomfield throughout her life, and how they strengthened community bonds.
** Social Activities, Recreation, Bloomfield, Community Bonds **
# Changes in Bloomfield Over the Decades
> Explores how Bloomfield has changed since the 70s, through significant developments and shifts in demographics, while maintaining many of its core qualities.
** Bloomfield History, Community Changes, Demographics **
# Race Relations and Inclusion
> Cindy discusses her experience with racial dynamics in Bloomfield and contrasts it with the broader and more challenging racial climates in other regions.
** Race Relations, Inclusion, Racial Dynamics, Bloomfield **
# Economic Development and Agricultural Heritage
> Discusses the importance of economic development in Bloomfield, the town’s agricultural background, and the balance of maintaining open land.
** Economic Development, Agricultural Heritage, Bloomfield **
# Housing Challenges and Accessibility
> Examines issues of housing accessibility in Bloomfield, touching upon discrimination, redlining, and personal experiences within the local real estate market.
** Housing, Accessibility, Discrimination, Real Estate **
# Bloomfield's Unique Community Appeal
> Concludes with Cindy expressing her love for Bloomfield’s unique qualities and magic, and her commitment to the town's community values.
** Community Appeal, Bloomfield, Unique Qualities **

story.description: The interview with Cindy Lloyd, conducted by Donna Wnuck, delves into a variety of topics surrounding Cindy's life, career, and experiences in Bloomfield. Cindy discusses her family background, including her father's journey into law enforcement and her own career path inspired by the community she grew up in. She reflects on the significant role Bloomfield played in shaping her identity, emphasizing the town's diversity and community spirit. Lloyd shares insights into her policing career, her efforts in youth engagement, and the challenges she faced, including gender discrimination. She also highlights the changes in Bloomfield over the decades, touching on issues such as housing access, race relations, and community activities. Throughout, Cindy expresses a strong attachment to and appreciation for Bloomfield, describing it as a unique and welcoming place.
story.title: Cindi Lloyd oral history.WAV
